comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
A) In light of the discussions of toxic environments and their effects on people, I've noticed a pattern whereby a lot of people will do spectacularly cruel and idiotic things in high school, and then regret it not that long after graduating; meanwhile, it's become an acknowledged cliche that everyone hates high school, and I've worked with teenagers and noticed that they become better people during the summer, when they aren't going to school.

Does this suggest to you that part of what makes the public education system so dysfunctional is that the entire system has gone septic on the inner planes? Obviously, some schools might be more or less clean on the inner planes, but it is striking that I've yet to meet anyone under about 40 who liked going to a North American high school; and that nearly everyone I've talked to knows what I mean when I talk about an oppressive atmosphere....

B) I'm noticing that as I put together ideas for planetary charity, I keep circling back to the same planets: my main targets are Venus, and Saturn, with Mercury and Mars in the middle, and I'm fairly light with ideas for The Moon, The Sun, and Jupiter.

I'm just getting into this, but as I reflect on this, I see it is hardwired into my personality: I tend towards a fairly difficult relationship with Venus and to a lesser extent Mars, and thus want to invoke them to get their help improving the areas of my life ruled by them; I easily see the strengths of Saturn and to a lesser extent, Mercury, and thus can easily come up with plenty of ways to call on those planets to help; while my relationship with Jupiter, The Sun, and The Moon are more neutral, so I neither need their help as much, nor can I as easily come up with ways to ask for their help. Are there concerns with doing planetary charity in an imbalanced fashion over the longer term? Should I make an effort to balance all of the planets?

C) This is a bit of a weird question, but I've noticed my circadian rhythm seems less affected by place than others'. Left to my own devices, I tend to get up and go to bed at around the same time (in absolute terms) no matter where I live; and the further I have to shift my schedule, the more unpleasant this is for me. This isn't a matter of having any kind of medical condition, because in some time zones I wake up far too early, and in others I end up staying awake far too late at night; nor is this a matter of jet lag, because I can be in a time zone for years and still my body clock wants to keep around the same rhythm.

Is there anything in the occult lore you can think of that could explain this? It's totally bizarre, and I've been completely unable to come up with any kind of materialistic explanation for it, despite having tried nearly my entire life.

comments.title: Dungeonomics and "Sign of the Cross" Equivalent Question
comments.content:
Good Evening,

If you have begun participating in the winter holiday season, may it be minimally stressful and maximally joyful. If not, may you be enjoying some cool, but not onerous weather.

To Share: Not real magical, but perhaps of interest to some here, this is a post using a) standard D&D assumptions, and b) economic theory to explain why wizards get into the sheep business: https://critical-hits.com/blog/2017/02/22/the-wizards-and-the-sheep/ Most fun if you are familiar with D&D, but hopefully amusing even if not.

To Ask: For JMG and the commentariat: I'm looking for gesture that is roughly equivalent to the sign of the cross - a physical self-blessing/prayer that can be used to open and/or close other prayers. Even if no specific gestures come to mind, if anyone knows the generic term for this sort of thing, that would be helpful for doing more research.

The things that the sign of the cross seems to have going for it in a Christian context are: recapitulates core symbolism of the faith, calls on the balanced aspects of deity, and touches/crosses the third eye, belly, and heart centers.

Edred Thorsson suggests a "sign of the hammer" for heathens in The Nine Doors of Midgard which is pretty much the same thing as the sign of the cross, but with an obviously different emphasis (touch forehead, solar plexus, left shoulder, right shoulder, crossing the heart in-between, at each spot call on a different deity or heiti of Odin). Divination suggests that this sign would be fine, but there might be something better out there to find/come up with, especially if I can find something that uses tree symbolism.

Might the opening to the Sphere of Protection qualify as something of the kind, or is it only appropriate for more ceremonial purposes?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone here for your time, thoughts, and care.

To any who will have them, I put forth my blessings and best wishes,
Jeff

replies.content: Probably not. Most organic substances, such as plastics, are diamagnetic rather than paramagnetic, and so will cancel out the paramagnetism of any powdered stone or clay that goes into them. Plain ceramic or natural stone is usually a much better choice.

If you have the chance to make something of concrete, see if you can get sand from a volcanic source -- basalt sand, for example -- and use that. It'll greatly increase the paramagnetism of the result.

comments.title: A question regarding Christian occultists
comments.content:
If I may ask a question for you and the rest of the commentariat who care to comment...

How do Christian occultists treat the prohibition in Deuteronomy against divination, witchcraft, magic and the like? Is it a matter of the prohibition no longer having any relevance with the coming of Jesus? Or is it a matter of whether magic is being using for “non-selfish reasons”?

(I’m aware that you’re not a Christian occultist, but if my memory is correct, you have mentioned that you have friends who are. Additionally, I believe you made reference to early (Middle Ages?) Christians often using magic, but I’ve never come across an (adequate?) explanation for how they reconciled their practices with the written prohibition)

replies.title: Re: A question regarding Christian occultists
replies.content:
It's quite simple. The Christian occultists I know recognize that there are many occult practices that their faith forbids, and they accept that prohibition. However, they also recognize that the Christian faith includes the gifts of the Holy Spirit, which can seem superficially similar to prohibited occult practices, in the eyes of those who have a narrow view of the faith. 1 Corinthians 12:1-11 is one relevant passage:

1 Now concerning spiritual gifts, brethren, I would not have you ignorant.
2 Ye know that ye were Gentiles, carried away unto these dumb idols, even as ye were led.
3 Wherefore I give you to understand, that no man speaking by the Spirit of God calleth Jesus accursed: and that no man can say that Jesus is the Lord, but by the Holy Ghost.
4 Now there are diversities of gifts, but the same Spirit.
5 And there are differences of administrations, but the same Lord.
6 And there are diversities of operations, but it is the same God which worketh all in all.
7 But the manifestation of the Spirit is given to every man to profit withal.
8 For to one is given by the Spirit the word of wisdom; to another the word of knowledge by the same Spirit;
9 To another faith by the same Spirit; to another the gifts of healing by the same Spirit.
10 To another the working of miracles; to another prophecy; to another discerning of spirits; to another divers kinds of tongues; to another the interpretation of tongues:
11 But all these worketh that one and the selfsame Spirit, dividing to every man severally as he will.

Such gifts as the word of knowledge -- that is to say, receiving knowledge from the Holy Spirit rather than from worldly sources -- the working of miracles, prophecy, and the discerning of spirits are superficially similar to occultism in their effects. To Christian occultists, however, these differ from the old Pagan occultism precisely because they come from the Holy Spirit, and are to be sought prayerfully through faith, study, spiritual disciplines, and (above all else) strict obedience to the commandments of Christ.

replies.title: Re: A question regarding Christian occultists
replies.content:
My view might differ from the standard Christian occultist view—I am either a Christian who looks at Christianity like a pagan, or a pagan who looks at paganism like a Christian—I can never figure out which.

Take a look at Numbers 5:11-31, and tell me that procedure is not blatant divination and magic. Also the references to lot-casting to find the will of God occur several times in numbers and Joshua, along with Chronicles.

You can tell me all the authors of the Bible agree with each other. You can tell me James and Paul agree on justification, for instance, but they plainly do not.

Jesus, on the other hand, talks like a magician, has no problem with extensive use of irony to make his points, and, if you read the sermon on the mount, he is essentially rattling off an extremely shrewd system magical (willfully consciousness-altering) ethics. “turn the other cheek” resonates remarkably with what magicians like Dion Fortune say regarding evil—you get the better of it not by engaging with it on its level, but by raising your consciousness above it and allowing it to implode. The humility he promoted, he ritually stitched into the fabric of reality with the events that ended his life on earth, with the consequence that it psychologically and spiritually destabilized the powers-that-were in Roman empire over the next five hundred years to make room for a new order. I ultimately see Jesus as a shrewd individual who saw the weak point in Roman thinking—their arrogant misappropriation of Jupiter energy as “might is right,” and his system of ethics and life as laid out in the gospels lead right into his magnum opus, the crucifixion and resurrection, where he used the energies of divine humility to alter the consciousness—and by extension—the mundane modus operandi— of the western world for the next two millennia. His work is the reason we still care about uplifting the weak, rather than merely evaluating them as pitiful and wretched from a distance (the classical attitude). His death and resurrection can be seen as the largest-scale successful magical working that we know of.

Additionally, the “prosopic union” of Nestorian Christology makes the gospels make much more sense to me. The authors of Mathew, Mark and Luke saw mainly his human hypostasis, whereas John, the beloved disciple, saw the prosopus of the Logos, the “building rule” of the fractal cosmos as someone put it last Monday, indwelling him to an extent that may never have occurred before or since in recorded history. The union was probably fluctuating in nature given that human consciousness is naturally fluctuating, and these fluctuations from greater divinity to greater humanity make a very interesting hermeneutic when brought to the gospels.

replies.title: Re: A question regarding Christian occultists
replies.content:
There's some excellent historical scholarship in support of the view thaat Jesus actually was (and possibly even thought of himself) as a magician. See, for example, Morton Smith's Jesus the Magician, which you can borrow and read (but not download) at www.archive.org See also Helen Ingram's website, http://wasjesusamagician.blogspot.com, which summarizes her doctoral dissertation in very readable form.

For one telling example, in the Gospel of John, when Jesus is brought forward for judgement, Pilate asks the accusers what crime they are accusing him of. Their answer (John 18:30) was that Jesus was a "kakòn poiôn [κακὸν ποιῶν]," that is, "one who does evil." The Latin Vulgate, and also the King James English Bible, translate this phrase by the single word malefactor, which more often than not refers specifically to a magician or witch, not to just any old evil-doing criminal.

replies.title: Re: A question regarding Christian occultists
replies.content:
One very well documented answer to your question is presented by Ft. Agostino Taumaturgo in his book Is Magic Wrong?

The gist of the argument, IIRC, is that the prohibitions in Deuteronomy were originally directed towards specific kinds of magic that delved into dark powers: not any kind of magic but witchcraft and goetia, not any kind of divination but necromancy, etc. It was later generations of clergymen who issued blanket prohibitions towards any kind of magic.

Please be aware that the author is an Christian occultist himself, with all the pros and cons it implies. On the one hand, the man's speaking from first hand experience. On the other hand, rationalization may be an issue.

In an effort to figure out what to expect in the years ahead, I've been mulling over which planet will rule Pluto's fading out. It seems quite likely to me that the planet in question is Saturn, for a few reasons: Saturn appears to play a role in changes in the planets, given it takes about a Saturn Return for planets to come in or out of focus; Saturn naturally rules the past, and as Pluto fades away He'll increasingly belong there; and division, which requires a limit to mark the boundaries, seems to me to be naturally Saturnine.

This, however, lead to a very, very uncomfortable thought: Pluto is still clinging to power because a very, very large number of people are unable to let go of the dream of Man's Destiny Among the Stars. In a real sense, all of the other manifestations of Pluto are being increasingly marginalized already, and if a major shock discredited the Myth of Progress, then all bets are off for everything, given how massive a shock it would be to societies and cultures worldwide; and given the effectively theocratic system of governance in the Western World, governments as well.

All it would take would be a major shock related to the space program to end it all then, and I have come up with a possibility which would fit with both Saturnine and Plutonian symbolism quite well: some poor fool(s) try to do a mission to Mars, and die horrifically along the way. It fits with the brutal , no mercy aspects of Saturn; doing something that stupid, the people involved would get the consequences.

Meanwhile, it would fit the Plutonian symbolism quite well as well: it would be shocking and likely a media spectacle; bring the dream of space travel, and quite plausibly the entire Plutonian Era, to a sudden, screeching halt; and it would mean that that mission, which will almost certainly start with a massive amount of fanfare, would fizzle out into a horrific mess, as the astronauts slowly died on the way to Mars; and the only way it can happen is if the people making the decision systematically marginalize their own scientific advisors, who almost certainly have been telling the politicians in response to any proposed manned mission, that it would be a death sentence.

What really bothers me now, is that in a real sense the Plutonian Era got started with World War I. Plutonian themes were around before, but they took hold in popular society largely as a result of the mess that World War I created; and it became clear how horrific World War I was going to be around the same time that Saturn reached the opposition point to its position in Pluto's discovery chart. By the same principle, it's plausible that whatever will lead to the marginalization of the remnants of the Plutonian likely started around when Saturn hit the opposition to its position in Pluto's demotion chart in February.

If we take this seriously, then the way that the US and China seem to be entering a new space race, with both sides promising to send astronauts to Mars in the near future, looks quite ominous indeed.

Does this seem plausible to you as well?

comments.content:
First of all, a little offering - this is a piece from my translation of Johann Baptist Krebs' Paths to immortality based on the undeniable powers of human nature which I've just finished the first draft of, and I thought you'd enjoy it:

"All that humans pursue and do with zeal comes to live in them and assumes dominance in them. Language, form, movement, and everything becomes subordinate to this force so that it finally possesses us entirely and thinks and acts in our ego.
Anyone who lets this new evil ego calmly prevail is led by it with cleverness and acumen through life, and they are finally accustomed to trust only earthly powers, and by contrast considering eternal properties and influences to be folk tales.
Such humans can only rise with effort to a sham faith, the power of which, however, is constantly closed to them. Contact with spirits is to them preposterous, an impossibility, and for any allusion to it they have an objection which is given to them without their knowing by the evil spirit produced in them. The quick gift of contradicting increases their obduracy in that they enjoy themselves in the shimmer of their contradictions and consider it to be a result of extreme rationality.
In our days is has come so far that faithless and rational are one and the same thing. But then as a result even doctors and philosophers no longer know how to help with the slightest spiritual phenomenon."

Now, on another note, in reading Karl Spiesberger's Magische Einweihung (originally published as Hermetisches ABC, Vol. I), he provides an explanation of why two people born in the same place at the same hour can turn out very differently despite having a near identical horoscope. His argument is that each will have brought in different karma and degrees of development from past lives, and that this explains a large part of the difference. Furthermore, he suggests that "the heavier the karmic entanglement, the more bound to fate the human is" [Je schwerer nun die karmische Verstrickung, desto schicksalsgebundener der Mensch] (p.230). He emphasises the importance of working on your character so that you can strengthen your freewill and so overcome these influences - "Alter your character and you alter your fate! Want — and the stars will obey you!" [Ändere Deinen Charakter und Du änderst Dein Schicksal! Wolle — und Dir gehorchen die Sterne!] (p.230 f.). Though he also suggests it is rare to fully escape the influence of the stars (and warns that no sailor throws the compass overboard regardless of how good the ship).

My question is whether this idea of the more karma the more the stars bind you could be considered reflective of a wide swathe of Western esoteric thought or whether it is less widely held.

comments.title: The ritual of the grail
comments.content:
Dear JMG

I have finished reading your outstanding new book Ceremony of the Grail. This joins so many dots, and opens so many mental doors I don't know where to begin, or more importantly, what forces will be renewed in the world.

A couple of things are puzzling me however.

1) The ritual of the grail, or at least, the one you've sketched out is certainly different to all other occult rituals I've seen. What egregors and power does it invoke? I ask only because of the many purported fears and dangers surrounding the grail, and my slight hesitation in seeing the ritual potentially used in a country and land that may have never received it at all. I don't want to annoy the powers in this land.

On this, I note that it seems similar to masonic rituals, in that the symbolism is universal, and not limited to any particular pantheon or system.

2) I am wondering, in this light, if the power of the ritual was more in the traditions and orders that protected and sustained the temple technology (with its very real earthly powers), and the initiation is more the test of character required to be worthy of that knowledge?

Or just that the rituals of the inner orders, that may have taught more explicit magic, are of course no longer extant?

replies.title: Re: The ritual of the grail
replies.content:
1) My reconstruction of the ceremony, as the book explains, draws extensively on early Masonic and para-Masonic rituals. The use of a universal symbolism is exactly the point -- that's why Masonry has spread worldwide with such success, and why many esoteric orders that use the same kind of ritual structure (such as Martinism) have been able to do the same thing. I'm fairly sure that many of the ancient mysteries used a similarly universal symbolism, which is why they traveled so well. The egregor the Grail ritual invokes is its own; the powers are whatever archetypal forces correspond to its egregoric patterns in the local magical and spiritual ecosystem.

2) Most esoteric orders don't teach magic. The broader realm of occultism contains many things, and magic is only one of them; nor is magic necessarily well suited to everyone. Meditation, occult study, and initiatory ritual are the core practices of the broader occult tradition. Magic, divination, and healing are among the specialties that some orders went in for and others did (and do) not. As for the power of the ritual, it's precisely in the symbolism, and develops over time when the initiate explores the ritual and its symbolism through meditation and study, awakening the corresponding forces in the deep places of his or her psyche.

(One of the things that's confused a lot of students of occultism in recent decades is the overemphasis given to magic by some Golden Dawn alumni, particularly Aleister Crowley and Israel Regardie. Even in the original GD, magic was much less central than it's since been made out to be -- the entire Outer Order curriculum involved only one magical ritual, the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram, and the Portal grade added a total of one more, the Middle Pillar exercise. The Inner Order required only a basic mastery of the Pentagram and Hexagram rituals, the Rose Cross ritual, and the consecration of the seven working tools of the 5=6; there was plenty of other work for those who wanted it, but many Adepti explored other aspects of occultism and did little ritual magic. It was a more balanced way to approach occultism; I'm hoping that at some point GD practitioners will consider moving back toward that approach.)

The M stands for "microwave".

It doesn't have to be (microwave-scale) regular electromagnetic fields you're building a resonator or waveguide or what-have-you for. Ordinary electromagnetic fields don't seem to have enough metaphorical stable degrees of freedom per point in space to work like what everyone says etheric fields work like. Trying to analyze etheric phenomena as if they were simple electromagnetic phenomena might be as impossible as trying to analyze chemical phenomena as if the only kind of matter that existed was hydrogen. (Maybe that's something you could partly do with a very contorted and fine-tuned effective field theory, where heavier elements were considered to be fortuitously stably mashed-together hydrogen atoms, but it would be better not to try.)

On the subject: I recently came across a sort of experimental mathematics project called "Lenia" (from Latin lenis, “smooth”) that's at the intersection of artificial life and soliton theory. It's an investigation of continuum generalizations of the curious sometimes ambulatory persistent patterns found in the Game of Life and other cellular automata. The shapes that the investigator, Bert Wang-Chak Chan, found, resemble the shapes I vaguely imagine etheric entities to have when they aren't really prioritizing spatial structure, even if the resemblance would be only very loose and qualitative. (All the patterns in Lenia are altered by its presupposition of a spatial frame of reference, which is an assumption that seems unlikely to generalize.) Chan is a little bit loose with terminology when he calls them "life-forms" since the space of variants on a given pattern is very small and so evolution isn't really an option.

comments.title: Minerva
comments.content:
My elderly father has been having health difficulties recently. I started to feel very down about it all and was finding it hard to come to terms with his increasing frailty. The other day, I spoke out loud and asked for help, from any god who might hear me, in easing his troubles. That very day, an offer of help came and was eagerly accepted. It has made a huge difference to everyone

Once the dust had settled from the surprise, I wondered which god had heard my plea and asked. The answer that came back was a firm ‘Minerva’, almost as if someone had spoken aloud. I’d heard her name before but had no idea who she is. Reading about her, I’m wondering if she’s been there all my life because she represents so much of what I’ve needed, ever since I was a little girl and had to learn far too young how to stand up for myself and then figure out how to deal with the mountain of resulting trauma. Sometimes I’m amazed that I’m actually alive and relatively sane, with a job I adore and much love in my life. Was Minerva there all along? Do gods do that, take an interest in people who aren’t aware of them?

Oh yes, as an aside, a short while after hearing 'Minerva' I logged into a forum I use and the first poster's name I saw was 'Sulis Minerva'. Also, I have for some years been keen on searching for little owl images in the paintings of artists such as David Teniers. It feels rather like I've been getting nudged.

To say thank you, I’ve ordered a little owl statuette and a lovely lapel pin of the same bird. This feels like a steep learning curve. Is there anything else to consider? I’ve done a lot of searching but most information out there is quite simplistic and repetitive. Maybe I should just ask Minerva herself.

My head is still spinning. Is this real? It feels real.

replies.title: Re: Minerva
replies.content:
Hi there,

This may or may not be helpful to you. The specific form of "Sulis Minerva" is a syncretization of the Roman Minerva with Sul/Sulis, a Celtic goddess associated at once with healing springs and the Sun. Following some of the titles given her in JMG's books, I address her as "Lady of the Threefold Fire," who unites the life-giving and life-destroying heat of the Sun; the chthonic fire that warms the healing springs; and the hearthfire that warms the home. I view her as the specifically Celtic form of the Divine Potency who appears as Minerva or Athena in other contexts.

I wrote the following prayer to her, which I've shared here before:

Hail Sulis, Lady of the Threefold Fire.

To thee I give thanks for the fire that descends from the heart of the Sun and warms the face of the living Earth.

Hail Sulis, Lady of the Threefold Fire.

To thee I give thanks for the fire that rises from the heart of the Earth and warms the waters of the healing springs.

Hail Sulis, Lady of the Threefold Fire.

To thee I give thanks for the fire that radiates from the hearth, and warms our homes and gladdens our hearts.

Hail Sulis, Lady of the Threefold Fire.

Holy Lady Sul, for all thy gifts and blessings I thank thee, and bless thee in turn. May  May I walk in thy divine hand this day and always.


I regularly ask her help specifically in blessing herbal teas, in which case I will end the prayer "...and may thy blessing be especially over this tea. May it be an offering unto thee, and may it bring [name requested result and person who will drink it.]"

Now again, I don't know if this will be of use to you-- given that the original name you received is "Minerva," it may be that her specifically Roman or Graeco-Roman forms would be more appropriate for you to work with. But I know that when I read your post I was relaxing on the couch reading this blog on my phone, and I felt a strong compulsion to get up and go to my computer so I could type this without, even though I'd just sat down a few minutes before and a cat had just fallen asleep on my lap. Such promptings are not lightly ignored!

replies.title: Re: Minerva
replies.content:
"Do gods do that, take an interest in people who aren’t aware of them?"

Not the OP - What a fascinating question... JMG do you know of any references/stories to Minerva as 'protector'?

***
Many of Minerva’s tales in Ovid are included in a series that show the vengeance and anger of the gods. As a virginal goddess, Minerva is not featured in the stories that center around love.

These ideas of transformation were not originated by Ovid. They played a major role in earlier mythology and the Latin poet expanded upon these themes.

Because they fit so well into the exiting framework, Ovid’s original stories are often included in modern retellings of classical mythology. In some cases Metamorphoses survived where older written legends did not, leading his stories to be passed down as primary sources though they were later inventions.
Because of this, the perception of Athena/Minerva has been highly influenced by her appearance in Metamorphoses. While she appears in earlier myths as a protective goddess, her wrath in Metamorphoses has led to her also being seen as a particularly vengeful and temperamental deity.

...Craftsmen in Britain embraced Minerva as a protective goddess to an even highly degree than they did in Rome. Many tools found from the period of Roman occupation show images of Minerva as the patroness of carpenters.

Jewellry from Britain often featured Minerva’s image and iconography as well. Hairpins, brooches, and signet rings from Roman Britain often showed Minerva in profile.

She was so popular in Britain that she was even featured in contexts that were entirely unique from her Roman worship. Coffins, for example, sometimes featured her imagery rather than more traditional Greco-Roman psychopomps.
https://mythologysource.com/minerva-roman-goddess/

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Ah, the Priory of Sion! It's one of the funniest stories in recent occult history. The guy who founded it, Pierre Plantard, was a con artist of a sort that used to be very common but is only found these days on the fringes of the occult scene. His gimmick was to gin up fake orders and sell memberships at a high price to suckers. The Priory of Sion was part of his stock in trade. You can get the whole story from this article by historian of religions Massimo Introvigne, and this trove of original documents. Thus the Priory didn't have any goals so exalted as you suggest; it was a moneymaking gimmick by a small-time French con man that got plucked out of its natural habitat and turned into a media sensation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I agree that Plantard can be easily dismissed as a con artist, but some other people from the PoS tradition (like Gino Sandri, Jean-Pierre Giudicelli de Cressac Bachelerie, Nicolas Haywood) are known as serious occultists. Maybe Plantard and the whole "Merovingian Bloodline" business were used by the unknown masters of the order to get some extra media attention, or as part of a misinformation campaign.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
At any given time, there are a variety of groups of people who are suffering and who have not already been championed, who could be made championed. At the time modern transgender activism began to gain traction, the group of people who were suffering because of involuntary gender identity dysphoria was probably emergently picked as a group to champion because of a few factors. By examining these factors, we can organize our speculation about some of the collective acts of will inherent in this process.

1) The suffering was relatively severe. The fraction of people who were suffering this way who, for example, committed suicide if the suffering was unrelieved, or even after it was relieved (though the blame for these further suicides is contested), was not that small.

2) Huge amounts of human behavior are shaped by signaling motivations to establish that one is one kind of person and not another kind of person. Often these imagined, intersubjective consensus kinds of person are unexamined and unconscious, and contain a lot of details that are historical or circumstantial accidents.

When I say "huge", for example, I mean that, from the perspective of supernatural consequences, on at least the middle astral level, drives for oneself or one's group to be seen as one kind of person and not another are probably even more powerful than sex drives.

There was a large cohort of potential activists and allies who had been acculturated, at first by college education and later also by social media, to orient their values in terms of a particular intersubjective consensus signaling hierarchy. This hierarchy doesn't have a good single name, but we can say it is associated with the culture of the present-day professional-managerial class. The hierarchy derives part of its polarity from an earlier generation's stereotyped experience of people becoming more wealthy and successful, and therefore more desirable to be associated with, the better-suited they were to working in interregional and international business. Part of being better-suited in this way is being more cosmopolitan and flexible in cognition and/or values, so that one is able to go on performing profit-producing business functions while navigating different demographics' sets of rules and cultural systems, without getting tripped up on rigidly defined moral or social categories that are provincial to one's original demographic.

Magically speaking, this part might mean that distinctions between gender categories, as an object of enmity, are serving partly as an egregoric-memory figure for distinctions defined by one's home culture that differ from distinctions defined by the culture where one has been sent to do business. Thus, they might also be a figure for distinctions between cultures themselves. The suffering that results from gender identity discrepancies might function partly as a figure for the economic deadweight loss caused by cultural barriers to arbitraging price discrepancies. The goal of gender-affirming surgery might function partly as a figure for the goal of industrial production coordination. The promotion of the recognition of spontaneously intersexed persons might function partly as a figure for the promotion of the recognition of multicultural enterprises or co-prosperity regions.

Furthermore, this signaling hierarchy's polarity also became aligned with progressive leftism, because the acculturation environment of college had become aligned with progressive leftism. Conservatism, especially conservative Christianity, became associated with dogmatism, inflexibility, unfitness for management, and fitness only for membership in the lower-middle or working classes. I don't understand why the associations would have had to not shake out in the opposite combination. It might have had something to do with Antonio Gramsci's project and the "long march through the institutions", or maybe it's because associating conservatism with success in international management is more credible when the management structure is an ethnically-based military empire rather than a commercial empire.

The Marxist connection might mean that gender distinctions are serving partly as a figure for the Marxist conception of false consciousness (as opposed to objective consciousness) or bourgeois class loyalty (as opposed to bourgeois class treason).

The people in this cohort were, partly consciously and partly unconsciously, on the lookout for a group to champion. They had the conscious idea that they were trying to be good people, tied together with the mostly conscious idea that they were trying desperately to be distinguished from conservative Christians, tied together with the unconscious idea that they were trying very desperately to be distinguished from people who would only qualify for lower-middle or working-class jobs, friend groups, spouses, or amounts of social power.

3) The Turchin cycle of elite overproduction was in its contraction phase. That might have added to the desperation.

4) Conservative Christianity had a pre-existing commitment to rigid gender categories and sexual orientation norms. Conservative Abrahamic religion seems to take all morally-loaded binary distinctions as a figure for each other, and all uncontrolled forms of liminality or category boundary fudging as figures for each other, so that if someone for example breaks a norm against wearing clothes with mixed fibers, it is as though this is a kind of subversion that threatens the stability of norms against spouses cheating or judges taking bribes. This more or less determines the interpretation of the implied extra meanings of scripture passages like Deuteronomy 22:5 or 1 Corinthians 6:9. But, in theory, this anti-subversiveness could have instead been turned into a rigid form of progressivism, like anti-fascism, or like the "left-conservatism" seen among those who grew up in early-to-middle Soviet Russia. I don't know why that would have had to not happen.

5) There were other similar groups of people who were still also suffering or were in danger of suffering, in the eyes of that cohort, because conservative Christianity had not been politically disempowered and morally discredited: homosexuals, bisexuals, women seeking historically male-occupied social roles, and women who did not wish to bear a conceived fetus. Championing people with gender identity dysphoria was arguably a kind of solidarity with that group, and refusing to champion people with gender identity dysphoria might have seemed like a kind of subversion or category boundary fudging that would threaten the stability of intersectional solidarity.

6) There was no particular population of people who might be clearly harmed, that the activist cohort hadn't already disregarded the preferences of on the grounds that this population had earned the punishment of having their preferences disregarded (arguably as an additional way of putting even more psychological distance along the binary axis between themselves and lower-middle-class-and-below conservative Christians), if norms were changed so that believing in the idea of a gender distinction was punished. Perhaps there would be a diffuse increase in health insurance prices.


Interestingly, gender identity dysphorias (and presumably other identity dysphorias such as bodily integrity identity, abledness identity, species identity, or whatever) are themselves also built out of signaling motivations to establish that one is one kind of person and not another kind of person. In the case of identity dysphorias, the details of how brain development and instinct wired up the motivation and the kind-of-person classifier together might be a bit different than the case of motivations to be seen as a psychologically competent candidate for professional-managerial-class social roles.


The main alternative approach on offer to gender dysphoria (discounting the unserious proposal of letting the suicides and life-long miseries just happen) seems to be the Christian idea of suffering without an attempt to control whether one is suffering, and becoming psychologically invested in a framing in which the suffering is meaningful and positive, in this case by willing to unite the suffering to that of Jesus. This is perhaps an inherently conservative attitude. A progressive might say that this Christian attitude acts to enable those who would motivatedly fudge the category distinction between suffering that is inherent to the human condition and suffering that an oppressive social structure is using to meaninglessly, life-negatingly perpetuate itself, in the direction of labeling a given form of suffering as inherent, to preserve an undeserved social position. A conservative might say that the modern gender activism attitude acts to enable those who would motivatedly fudge the category distinction in the other direction, to acquire or jealously destroy a social position they don't already have.


Overall there are a great many similitudes, analogies, and figurations in this situation, that might have some magical implications. The deep instinctive relationship between gender distinction identities and support-versus-opposition-to-gender-distinction distinction identities is especially full of unfamiliar potential consequences. But it doesn't resemble anything I'm used to thinking about, except partly shadow projection.

replies.title: Re: Shadow Work

replies.title: Re: Shadow Work
replies.content:
I don't know how compatible they are with GD magic, but a few options for Shadow work:

1) Owning Your Own Shadow by Robert A. Johnson - this is pretty brief and straightforward and includes some exercises for figuring out what you have pushed into your Shadow and how to come to terms with it.

2) King, Warrior, Magician, Lover by Robert Moore - This is mostly useful if you are a man, but it describes the four archetypes in the title, and relevant for Shadow work, what they look like when they are expressed as part of your Shadow rather than healthily (Tyrant, Sadist/Masochist, Manipulator, and the Addicted)

3) The Shadow and Evil in Fairy Tales Marie-Louise von Franz - I haven't actually read this one, just skimmed it, but von Franz was a student of Jung's who did a lot of work on exploring archetypes (including the Shadow) in folklore. Might be helpful.

4) Jordan Peterson (either various videos/podcasts or chapter 6 of 12 Rules for Life) - Peterson is very big on integrating your Shadow, which is unsurprising since he's heavily influenced by Jung. Rule 6 of his book "Set Your House in Perfect Order before You Criticize the World" is an examination of the role of resentment and bitterness in our psyches. His advice is to pay attention to where you are resentful or bitter (or where you fantasize resentfully or bitterly) and realize that that is likely a pointer to parts of your Shadow and explore from there. He also recommends noticing what people/things in the world anger or disgust you, as it is very likely you are projecting some of your shadow on them if you have that reaction.

Hope some of these help, and good luck!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Is Santa real?

So yes, he's real, and none of the planetary powers can be called "good" or "evil" -- they simply are.

comments.title: Shadow work
comments.content:
What is shadow work, and how do you do it?
Sorry for these basic questions, I just think your a reliable source

replies.title: Re: Shadow work
replies.content:
Your Shadow, in Jungian psychology, is the sum total of everything that's true about you that you don't want to deal with, and so project onto other people: "I'm not the one who is X, it's that other nasty person over there who's X!" Look at fundamentalist preachers ranting about homosexuality as a great example -- you know as well as I do that they're frantically trying not to admit to themselves that they're attracted to men.

Shadow work is the process by which you stop projecting your shadow onto other people and admit the truth about yourself to yourself. There are many ways to do it; I recommend asking people who are into Jungian work (for example, the commentariat on r/Jung over on Reddit) for suggestions.

comments.title: Tarot
comments.content:
JMG and commentariat,

I have been trying to learn divination using Tarot for a while now, I have worked my way through the authors I am aware of, Eden Gray, Dion Fortune, Gail Fairfield,and others and have settled for the time being on Robert Wang's Quabalistic Tarot.

I have two nagging question that keep surfacing, first is the source of power behind the way the cards show up. None of the authors I have read seem to address this. I do my best to shuffle the cards to randomness, yet the results seem anything but random. Am I simply being fooled? is my subconscious somehow influencing them ? The Astral Light ?

The second question is interpretation, I find the more recent authors to be too "squishy" for my taste, the 'whatever you want the cards to mean" approach while the Golden Dawn and older traditions seem to take a more "fire and brimstone" approach that implies "hard"implications that you are stuck with.

I have found Wang's book to be the most thorough discussion of the Quabala and Tree of Life i have found yet, but in the chapter on divination he uses the golden Dawn interpretations of the cards.

any comments or insight will be most appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Tarot
replies.content:
1) There are very few actually random events in the universe. The patterns made by shuffling cards aren't among them; your subconscious can easily manipulate that process.

2) I'm a little confused here. You're saying that you don't like the modern authors because they're too squishy and yet you don't like the GD system because it's not squishy enough! If you don't want hard implications that you're stuck with, then you're basically going to end up thinking the cards mean what you want them to mean, and if you aren't willing to take that approach, then now and again you're going to be faced with hard implications that you're stuck with.

My advice? Accept the hard implications. Life is like that, you know -- very often things happen to you and you just have to live with them. A divination system that reflects that will be more likely to give you good advice.

replies.title: Re: Tarot
replies.content:
Ah! Many thanks for that reality check! The squishy approach is kind of useless, is a more useful way to use the cards to say "Ok, these are the forces that seem to be present and active at the moment, what are they trying to tell me, and how do I deal with them?"

Thank you again for the incredible work you do.

I'm wondering whether you have any opinion about the sigil technique beloved of chaos magicians, namely to write down your intent such as "It is my will to find a new job in a month", then cross out any repeating letters (some say to cross out vowels also), then rearrange the remaining letters into a sigil. You then "launch" the sigil through orgasm, intense meditation, exercise, etc. while staring at it (some even use methods such as mailing a copy of the sigil to random strangers).

Gordon White recommends a technique called "shoaling" where you launch a bunch of sigils at once, but you have one of them be a sigil for something you absolutely know will come true, such as "It is my will to see a McDonald's" when you know you drive by one each day. The idea is it primes the others to come true as well.

Originally this is supposed to be (loosely) based on a technique by A.O. Spare. Do you think this has any validity/effectiveness?

replies.title: Re: Chaos Magick Sigil Technique
replies.content:
I've never worked with it. I read several early chaos magic works, notably Peter Carroll's Liber Null and Psychonaut (which I picked up when it was first published); I also read the Illuminatus! trilogy when it was hot off the presses -- that's the not-always-acknowledged source of a lot of chaos magic ideas -- and I've also read Spare, but that style of magic really never turned my crank. Call me an old fuddy-duddy if you like, but I prefer the structure and the established egregor of the older, more formal styles of magic.

All of which amounts to saying, "Good question. It's not something I practice."

comments.title: Gramsci and the occult dimensions of class struggle
comments.content: Greetings Archdruid! I know that you are not a big fan of Marxism, but, have you ever come across the Prison Notebooks of Antonio Gramsci? He was an unorthodox Marxist theoretician, in that he was an outspoken critic of economic determinism and mechanistic materialism which are inherent in the mainstream Marxism. He was not an occultist or mystic per se, but somehow he intuited the immaterial dimensions of class struggle. Many of his writings emphasize the importance of struggle for counter-hegemony in the "superstructure" (culture, religion, art, media, philosophy etc.) instead of placing too much focus on economical and technological "substructure/base".

replies.title: Re: SRIA prayer for healing
replies.content:
Here you go. It's in resources/archives/prayers meditations and rituals --

https://sria.org/a-simple-ritual-for-lighting-candles-which-may-be-used-for-healing-and-other-purposes

0=0, by the way, is a Golden Dawn grade of initiation. The 0=0 sign is made by bringing your hands up to the sides of your head, the palms facing forward, and then stepping forward with your left foot and flinging your hands forward, pointing the fingers at the target. Think of going "ZAP!" and you've got the right idea. Then draw back the left foot, let the right hand fall to your side, and bring your left index finger to your lips. Think of going "Sssshhhh!" and you've got the right idea.

You can do the candle ritual at any time, and it can be done as often as you like.

comments.title: Journaling
comments.content: Many times I have seen you recommend journaling for people struggling with internal personal issues. Do you have any particular journaling techniques or approaches to recommend? I have been doing freeform journaling off and on for some decades and it always seems to arrive at the same result: I get in touch with my parentification issues (basically realizing at around age 12 that I was saner than either of the supposed adults raising me and I was the closest thing to a responsible adult in the house), remember how much I wanted to be kidnapped/rescued by one or more trustworthy, caring adults, cry about that, return to my adult awareness that I did, in fact, get away from that situation and don't need rescuing (which only feels more distressing because it means that nobody is coming) and go on with my life, only to have the same thing crop up again the next time I hit a rough spot. If there's some other approach to this practice that would make it possible for me to move on, emotionally, I would very much like to hear it.

replies.title: Re: Journaling
replies.content:
Okay, then you've gotten as much out of journaling on that particular issue as you're likely to get. If you know what your issues are, that's an important step. What comes next is the hard task of acceptance. The next time the same business comes up, consider facing it squarely and saying, "Yeah, I know. That was a long time ago. Suck it up and deal" -- and then genuinely accept that nobody is going to rescue you but you. I don't know if that will work for you, but the equivalent certainly did for me.

I wonder, though, if there are other issues. You might try journaling about the deeper question: why do you keep circling around that particular set of issues? Again, that was a long time ago...

Have you seen this mess, where someone bought a bunch of souls from people?

https://web.archive.org/web/20210304163032/https://liminalwarmth.com/the-questionable-ethics-of-buying-and-selling-souls/

The actual contract is quite disturbing as well. Here is a link to it:

https://web.archive.org/web/20210303023045/https://liminalwarmth.com/contract-for-sale-of-soul/

A few things stand out to me, as an occultist in training who also works with law: the person behind this explicitly tried to think like a demon, and cover all possible loopholes and ways out; which it seems to me may mean that a demon got into the person's head, and helped draft the contract. If this is the case, then it seems plausible that said demon is going to be able to take advantage of this to get souls.

The preamble, spelling out which courts have jurisdiction, includes a provision you never include in a contract, of any sort, namely allowing any jurisdiction to claim authority to hold trials. The reason why these preambles always state one jurisdiction is simply that everywhere does things a little differently, and by authorizing any and every jurisdiction to hear cases about these contracts, this almost guarantees that some very sleazy "courts" will hear cases about it. Including a monetary transfer will, at least under most forms of Earthly law, make it a binding contract.

Section I F explicitly stats the seller may not believe in the existence of souls, and the buyer is taking the risk associated with this. This kind of legal language could be used, in some circumstances, to get around the typical protections granted by not knowing something, as it could be argued that the seller here is explicitly acknowledging that they don't believe in souls, and thus the existence of the soul does not change the nature of the contract they signed.

Some of the provisions of Section IV A, where the uses are outlined, seem quite disturbing to me, since they effectively allow for the buyer to transfer any kind of energies they want onto the souls, and permit the buyer to remove the souls from the usual cycle of reincarnation, and allow for the buyer to transfer the soul to anyone else they deem fit; IV C&D bind the seller against taking any actions to try to reclaim their soul, which, paired with E&F seem to force the seller into protecting the buyer against actions by others to reclaim their soul; finally, section IV M is so over-broad that most courts will throw it out: it is impossible for one's actions not to be influenced by others, and since the sections does not have any sever-ability clauses embedded in it, the whole thing will likely be thrown out, leaving the rest, most of which explicitly does have severability clauses, applying.

Section V H explicitly requires the seller acknowledge that should they not believe in a soul, they are still bound by the terms of the contract; while the rest of Section V ensures that the seller has no ability to resist whatever the buyer chooses to do, and in fact that the seller explicitly renounces any options to appeal to anybody who does not recognize the contract as binding for aid.

What do you think will happen to the poor fools who signed this contract? It seems likely to me that they just sold their souls to demons, but this might take a while for them to find out....
